By doubling the length while keeping all else equal, we effectively halve the number density of coils per unit distance along the spring axis. Consequently, this means more energy must be expended to stretch any given segment equivalently because fewer segments contribute simultaneously under load. Therefore, the correct answer aligns perfectly with option B—four times the initial force being necessary to double the extension of the spring.

raw_response:
I agree with the conclusion presented in Rounds 1 and 2. The key point here is understanding how the spring constant changes with respect to the physical dimensions of the spring. By doubling the length while keeping all else equal, we effectively halve the number density of coils per unit distance along the spring axis. Consequently, this means more energy must be expended to stretch any given segment equivalently because fewer segments contribute simultaneously under load. Therefore, the correct answer aligns perfectly with option B—four times the initial force being necessary to double the extension of the spring. 

Answer: B Your agreement adds an additional layer of clarity on why the force requirement increases exponentially rather than linearly with increased length, emphasizing the inverse relationship between effective spring constant and length expansion. Given these points, my final answer remains consistent with Option B. Answer: **B**.
